data.title: I'm Buying Up To 9% Yield For Recurring Income
data.description:
Those familiar with the “4% rule” know that this academic theory suggests that you can safely withdraw 4% from your portfolio every year to fund your lifestyle in retirement. It’s worth noting, however, that this rule came out in 1994, when inflation was lower than where it is today. Plus, at that time, the S&P 500 (SPY) was also far less concentrated compared to the “Magnificent 7” stocks of today, and carried a far higher yield than the 1.2% yield today.

Moreover, the overall market valuation appears to be stretched at present with the S&P 500 carrying a P/E ratio of 30.6 and The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) forecasting a lost decade for stocks due to high valuations. As such, counting on capital appreciation on the S&P 500 every year to make up for the low dividend yield may cause some investors to eat more into their principal than they would like.

This brings me to the following 2 picks, which provide yields in the 6% to 9% range. This beats the 4% rule without investors having to time the market and dip into principal to fund their income needs. Both are quality names in their respective industries, so let’s explore what makes them solid picks for recurring income!

#1: Hercules Capital, Inc. (HTGC)

Hercules Capital is one of a handful of internally managed BDCs, whose interests are better aligned with that of shareholders. That’s because shareholders own not only the underlying assets, but also the management platform.

HTGC invests in growth sectors including technology and life sciences. It has debt investments worth $3.5 billion across 118 portfolio companies. It also has warrant and equity holdings in 98 and 72 companies respectively with a value of $189 million. Quality of management matters, as HTGC has consistently outperformed the BDC peer group both in terms of ROAA (return on average assets) and ROAE (return on average equity) over the past 5 years, as shown below.

This, combined with a durable and growing dividend yield to result in outperformance over peers. As we can see below, HTGC has produced a stellar 119% total return over the trailing 7-year period, far outpacing the 48% of the BDC peer average.

Meanwhile, HTGC demonstrated strong performance in its 2024 results, including a record full year total investment income of $494 million, representing a 7.1% YoY increase. Net investment income also grew by 7.2% YoY to a record $326 million. HTGC also made record gross fundings of $1.8 billion last year, which is up by 13% from 2023, as demand returned. This makes a significant milestone as it marks $20 billion in cumulative fundings since HTGC’s inception.

Moreover, HTGC continued to generate appealing returns on investment with a GAAP effective yield of 13.7%. Portfolio credit quality was stable with an internal credit rating of 2.26 (on a scale from 1 to 5, with 1 being the highest quality). This represents just a 0.02x change both sequentially and YoY from 2.24 in both periods. Investments in non-accruals also remained low, at just 0.5% of portfolio fair value.

HTGC’s NAV per share grew by $0.23 last year to $11.66. NAV per share has fully recovered since hitting a low in 2022 due to higher interest rates and the associated mark-to-market asset right downs at that time.

Looking ahead, management is optimistic around the potential for increased M&A and capital markets activity this year, particularly as it relates to technology-focused businesses. While investment spreads have declined over the past 12 months due to lower interest rates, HTGC has a strong balance sheet to offset some of that yield compression.

This is reflected by its statutory debt-to-equity ratio of 76%, sitting well below the 200% regulatory limit for BDCs. As such, HTGC has the ability to leverage up to make more investments and offset declines in base rates. Moreover, HTGC has a wholly owned RIA subsidiary which generates around $1.9 million in quarterly dividends per quarter.

Importantly for income investors, HTGC’s base quarterly dividend of $0.40 is well-protected by net investment income with a 123% coverage ratio. This enabled HTGC to declare another $0.07 special dividend this year, which translates to a 9% total dividend yield based on the current share price of $21.27.

Admittedly, HTGC isn’t cheap by traditional BDC valuation standards with a price-to-NAV of 1.82x. However, I continue to believe that well-run internally managed BDCs should be evaluated based on their earnings power than their book value. With a forward PE of 10.8x, HTGC is trading at an earnings yield of 9.3%, which is appealing for this BDC with a strong balance sheet, safe portfolio metrics, and a solid track record of shareholder returns. As such, HTGC continues to make for an appealing high-yielding BDC.

Risks to HTGC include the potential for lower interest rates, which could compress its yields on debt investments. However, with inflation hitting 3% in January, lower rates appear to be off the table for now. Other risks include a macroeconomic downturn due to inflation or tariffs, which could pressure borrowers. Moreover, higher competition for deals could reduce investment yields.

#2: NNN REIT, Inc. (NNN)

NNN REIT remains one of my favorite net lease REITs alongside peers Realty Income Corporation (O) and Agree Realty Corporation (ADC). It has a long operating history and through balance sheet conservatism, it weathered the Great Recession of 2008-2009 and the pandemic of 2020 without the need to issue dilutive equity. Notably, NNN’s executive team has an average tenure of 21 years at the company.

At present, NNN has a portfolio of 3,568 properties leased to 385 tenants in 37 lines of trade. Its properties are well-diversified by geography across the U.S. with the South, Southeast, and Midwest being the top 3 segments representing nearly three-quarters (74%) of annual base rent. NNN also maintains strong occupancy, with it having never dipped below 96.4% over the past 20 years, while carrying a 98.3% average, as shown below.

NNN demonstrated steady 2024 results, with AFFO per share growing by 2.8% over the prior year period. It also maintained a high occupancy level at 98.5% and has a long weighted average remaining lease term of 9.9 years. In 2024, NNN was also active in recycling capital by disposing of 41 properties for $149 million, which produced $42.3 million of gains at a cap rate of 7.3%.

Those proceeds were put into $565 million worth of acquisitions at a higher cap rate of 7.7%. This included $217 million in property investments during Q4 alone at an initial cap rate of 7.6% with a portfolio-leading weighted average lease term of 20 years.

Recent risks to NNN include some tenant troubles including Frisch’s and Badcock Home Furniture. NNN has initiated evictions on Frisch’s stores and has so far re-possessed 33 of the 64 stores. In total, Frisch’s represents 1.5% of NNN’s ABR. With regard to Badcock, NNN has been able to re-lease 5 properties at a 70% rent recovery ratio and sell six properties at a 5.1% cap rate while redeploying proceeds into 7.6% cap rate acquisitions, resulting in 49% higher rent.

Importantly, NNN carries a strong balance sheet with a net debt-to-gross assets ratio of 40.5%, sitting below the 50% safe mark, a strong fixed charge coverage ratio of 4.2x, and a safe net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 5.5x, sitting below the 6.0x level considered safe for REITs. These factors support NNN’s BBB+ credit rating with a stable outlook from S&P.

Recent share price weakness since October has pushed NNN’s dividend yield up to 5.7%. The dividend comes with a safe 70.5% payout ratio and 35 consecutive years of raises, which is the third longest amongst publicly traded REITs.

Lastly, I find NNN to be attractive at the current price of $40.44 with a forward P/FFO of 12.0, which as shown below, sits far below its normal P/FFO of 16.1.

NNN also trades at a meaningful discount compared to peers Realty Income and Agree Realty, which carry P/FFO ratios of 13.1x and 16.8x, respectively. It appears the market is assigning NNN a lower valuation due to perceived recent headwinds due to tenant troubles, which management is actively working to resolve.

In the long run, I believe recent issues will be just a blip for NNN. With a near-6% yield and my expectations for around 5% annual FFO/share growth due to internal and external growth, NNN could reasonably produce market-beating total returns from the current price. As such, income and value investors could do well by picking up NNN at its current discounted price.

Investor Takeaway

Hercules Capital and NNN REIT both offer attractive high-yield investment opportunities that provide reliable income streams while mitigating the need for investors to dip into their principal, especially in an uncertain market environment. HTGC delivers strong shareholder-aligned management, solid portfolio credit quality, and an attractive 9% dividend yield backed by robust investment income.

Meanwhile, NNN offers a secure 5.7% yield that’s backed by 35 consecutive years of dividend raises. It trades at a discount to peers despite its strong financials and management’s proactive approach to tenant challenges. Together, HTGC and NNN present a balanced income strategy, with HTGC benefiting from growth in technology and life sciences, while NNN provides a defensive real estate play with consistent cash flow. As such, income investors could do well over the long run while capturing 6% to 9% yields from these two quality stocks.

data.title: SoftBank Corp.: Resilient Core, Risky AI Push
data.description:
SoftBank Corp. (OTCPK:SFBQF), the telecom subsidiary 40% owned by parent company SoftBank Group Corp. (OTCPK:SFTBY), is currently the third-largest by subscriber base in Japan. In its current form, the group is split into five segments – Consumer (mobile & broadband), Enterprise (enterprise-level communications services & solutions), Media & EC (commerce & advertising), Financial (payments), and Others.

At its core, though, this is a telco facing quite a few challenges in the face of a disruptive new entrant, Rakuten Group, Inc. (OTCPK:RKUNY) Mobile. Yet, pricing pressure and subscriber churn have so far been well under control. Meanwhile, the non-telco parts of the portfolio are as interesting as it’s ever been; on the one hand, you have payments subsidiary PayPay inching closer to a value-unlocking IPO event, and on the other, you have the company leading the GenAI charge, alongside OpenAI, in Japan.

In light of these new developments and with the stock rallying strongly since I last covered the name (see SoftBank: A Reliable Dividend Yielder With Compelling New Growth Engines), enough has changed to warrant a revisit.

Recapping a Solid Quarter All-Around

SoftBank Corp. was out with its fiscal Q3 numbers last week and for the most part, it was quite solid. At the group level, quarterly operating income was up +8.5% YoY on “record high” revenues. And on a year-to-date basis, operating income growth is running at +12.3% YoY – already at 86.5% of revised full-year forecasts.

Drilling down by segment, growth was encouragingly broad-based. The all-important Consumer segment led the way on a continued mobile recovery, underpinned by both a “stabilized” ARPU (or ‘average revenue per user’) and an “increase in subscribers” in mobile. Broadband (“increase in subscribers”) and mobile device sales (“reduction in discounts”) also contributed to the solid segment performance. All in all, a testament to management’s efforts in an increasingly competitive telco landscape post-Rakuten entry and amid promotional pressures from peers like NTT DoCoMo (DCMYY).

Elsewhere, top and bottom-line growth was also up in the enterprise, distribution, and media & EC segments. The financial segment (PayPay), on the other hand, was the key surprise. While still in ‘growth mode’, it is already seeing profits inflect higher. Scale is clearly playing a big role here, though cost discipline (management cited outsourcing as one lever) also helped to offset ongoing customer acquisition costs. This leaves the ‘other’ segment as the only one incurring operating losses currently.

New OpenAI Venture Adds Uncertainty

Now, SoftBank’s ‘other’ business may be loss-making today, but this segment, housing the company’s AI initiatives, also has the highest growth potential. How long the AI cash burn will last is anyone’s guess, though a recent joint venture with OpenAI to roll out an agent-based enterprise AI solution, “Cristal Intelligence” in Japan, indicates there is at least a path to monetization.

To be clear, the initial phase will entail more, not less, cash outflow, given that SoftBank will pay OpenAI a hefty $3.0bn/year for Cristal. It will also be a consolidated SoftBank Corp. subsidiary, so expect some negative near-term P&L impact as a result. On the flip side, the company does get exclusive marketing rights for Cristal Intelligence in Japan. It also gets to accrue future P&L upside, which per management, will be driven by usage fees.

For now, though, there isn’t a whole lot of information, either on SoftBank Corp.’s stake in the SoftBank Corp./SoftBank Group JV intermediary company or on the rollout timeline. Thus, it’s far too early to pencil in any mid-to long-term projections, though I’d watch closely for new disclosures in the quarters to come.

Also, Watch Out for AI-Driven Outlays Elsewhere

The OpenAI announcement follows SoftBank Corp.’s plans to develop its own proprietary large language AI model. Management has earmarked around JPY150bn of investments for this, which will cover NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) GPUs, as well as other equipment and expenses. Note, however, that this headline figure doesn’t include capex related to converting Sharp Corporation's (OTCPK:SHCAY) Sakai plant into a 150MW data center by 2026. Nor the goal to eventually increase this to 250MW in the longer term.

To be clear, I’m not suggesting a dilution event anytime soon, as SoftBank Corp. has the balance sheet capacity to cover data center costs to post the JPY200bn fund raise last year. But consider that losses in the ‘other’ segment are likely to increase as the company doubles down on GenAI and overruns seem inevitable, given the intense AI competition globally. Plus, the core mobile segment, essentially the funding source for SoftBank’s growth push, isn’t entirely out of the woods with regard to domestic competition. In sum, I would be a lot more cautious about AI than the market seems to be right now.

Summing Up

SoftBank Corp. has got a lot of things right over the last year. But cash-burning new ventures into AI adds quite a bit of risk to the investment case and dividend while intensifying competition in the core telco business remains a longer-term concern. In the meantime, the premium ~19x forward earnings multiple means things also need to go very right for the stock to keep moving higher. Net-net, I’m moving to the sidelines here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: China's Gold Market Update: Central Bank Purchases Continue In January
data.description:
Looking ahead

  • We anticipate continued strength in bar and coin demand while the soaring gold price may weigh on gold jewellery sales in tonnage terms – although consumer spending may not change much. And in the longer-term, a pilot programme that allows Chinese insurers to buy physical gold should provide additional support for investment demand.
  • And judging from the surging attention consumers are paying to gold, there is a possibility that wholesale demand in the near term – driven mainly by investment purchases – may stay stable before it cools as Q2, historically an off-season of gold consumption in China, approaches.

Gold kicked off 2025 with a sizable gain

Gold prices surged during the first month of 2025: the LBMA gold price PM in USD jumped 8% while the SHAUPM in RMB rose 5% (Chart 1). A strengthening local currency and fewer trading days due to the Chinese New Year (CNY) holiday between 28 January and 4 February led to the relative underperformance of the RMB gold price.

Our analysis shows that heightened geopolitical risks – such as the Trump administration’s tariff policies – improving gold ETF inflows and rekindling inflation concerns were main contributors to the record-shattering gold price in January.

Chart 1: Gold starts 2025 on a strong note

Annual returns of the SHAUPM in RMB and the LBMA Gold Price PM in USD*

Wholesale demand improved m/m but stayed weak y/y

China’s gold demand improved during the CNY month. On the wholesale side, 125t of gold were shipped out from the SGE, a 3% m/m rise (Chart 2). Seasonal stock replenishment from jewellery retailers, banks and other market participants ahead of the CNY holiday – a traditional peak season for gold consumption in China – was a main contributor.

Chart 2: Seasonal replenishment drives up wholesale demand m/m

Gold withdrawals from the SGE and the Shanghai-London gold price spread*

But our pre-holiday field research in Shenzhen, the hub of China’s gold jewellery wholesaling and manufacturing, indicates weaker-than-usual sentiment among gold jewellery retailers. With the gold price soaring and the past year’s demand picture reminding them of potential sustained weaknesses, gold jewellery retailers lowered their expectations for the holiday sales and stocked up less than previous years. And this is also reflected in the 54% y/y decline in January’s wholesale gold demand – but it is important to note that 2024 saw the strongest January in history – and it was 37% below the ten-year average (Chart 3).

Chart 3: Yet the seasonal m/m improvement can’t mask the y/y weakness

Gold withdrawals from the SGE and the 10-year average*

Chinese gold ETF flows flipped negative in January

Chinese gold ETFs lost RMB2.8bn (US$399mn) in January, equivalent to a 4.7t reduction in holdings (Chart 4). After the month’s outflow, their total AUM fell to RMB70bn (US$9.8bn) and collective holdings reached 110t. We believe the outflow can be mainly attributed to profit-taking activities ahead of the CNY holiday to avoid additional volatilities from international markets while the local market is closed.1 Meanwhile, improving investor risk appetite amid the stronger-than-expected Q4 and 2024 GDP data releases in the month may also have led to outflows from gold, the safe-haven asset.2

Chart 4: Chinese gold ETFs saw outflows in January

Collective holdings and monthly changes of Chinese gold ETFs*

China’s official gold holdings rose three months in a row

The PBoC announced another gold purchase to start 2025: the 5t increase lifted China’s official gold holdings to 2,285t, accounting for 5.9% of total foreign reserves (Chart 5). As noted in our recently published Gold Demand Trends report, China announced a total of 44t gold purchases during 2024 despite its six-month pause in the middle of the year. And we believe the PBoC’s move could have a positive impact on sentiment among local gold investors, as indicated by past anecdotal evidence.

Chart 5: The PBoC reported its third consecutive monthly gold purchase in January

Reported official gold holdings and gold as a percentage of total foreign exchange reserves*

Gold imports ended 2024 with a y/y decline

December saw China import 84t of gold, concluding Q4 with a total of 270t, based on the most recent data from China Customs. While Q4 imports fell 14% y/y, they rose more than 160% q/q – the y/y weakness and q/q seasonal strength are in line with our observation of China’s gold demand during the quarter.

In 2024, China imported 1,225t of gold (Chart 6), a 14% y/y decline and 16% below the pre-COVID five-year average (1,460t). And we saw similar trends in China’s gold consumption in 2024, which fell 10% compared to 2023, leading to the decrease in imports.

Chart 6: Gold imports remain below pre-COVID levels

Gold imports and averages pre-COVID*

Looking ahead

The CNY holiday between late January and early February showed signs of consumption revival. For instance, box office revenues during the holiday surged to a historical high and consumer spending on dining and travelling both rose compared to the same period in 2024.3

Gold consumption was also booming. Various information indicates that gold jewellery stores were busy during the holiday. Meanwhile, the climbing gold price continued to push consumers to lighter products.4

And the investment story is a much stronger one. The strong gold price performance, the central bank’s continued purchasing announcements and the local currency volatilities kept lifting investor attention for gold. Currently, the online searches for gold topped their previous peak seen in 2013 (Chart 7), when gold demand in China surged to the highest in history. Our conversations with market participants indicate that gold bar sales maintained their stunning pace seen in 2024, even leading to inventory shortages for some.

Chart 7: Consumer sentiment towards gold reached a record high

Baidu Search Index of the keyword “Gold”*

We believe the current trend may continue. In tonnage terms, demand for gold jewellery may stay tepid – although value-preserving motives will provide some support – but bar and coin sales should remain hot – and any gold price adjustment could be viewed as a good opportunity to enter. Lastly, we believe a recent announcement of policy changes that allows ten Chinese insurers to buy up to 1% of each company’s total assets in physical gold, as a pilot run, should provide longer-term support for local investment gold demand.5

Footnotes

1Local financial markets were closed due to the CNY holiday between 28 January and 4 February 2025.

2For more, see: China's fourth-quarter GDP grows at 5.4%, beating market expectations | 16 January 2025.

3For more, see: 消费迎来“开门红”！春节期间零售和餐饮同比增长4.1%，以旧换新首批810亿元资金已下达_腾讯新闻 | 8 February 2025.

4For more, see: 2025春节黄金市场热潮：小克重产品爆红，消费者观望情绪升温_金价_变化_央行| 7 February 2025.

5For more, see: China launches pilot program for insurance fund investments in gold - Chinadaily.com.cn| 8 February 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
